posts.name: Bob Hughes
posts.cooked:
• 2011 Gérard Boulay Sancerre Clos de Beaujeu - France, Loire Valley, Upper Loire, Sancerre (12/22/2015)
    Light yellow-gold in the glass; definitely a grassy note on the nose here.

Light-bodied on the palate, decent persistence and acidity here, yet more straightforward than the 2013 was the other day. Just opened, so maybe it fleshes out with some air. Nothing objectionable, but nothing really noteworthy either.

  • 2013 Gérard Boulay Sancerre Clos de Beaujeu - France, Loire Valley, Upper Loire, Sancerre (12/21/2015)
    Light yellow-gold in the glass; sea scents and a grassy/hay note as well on the nose.

Expansive and “rounded” on the palate, with decent supporting acidity. Tasty (very technical descriptor, right!). Saline, with nice persistence - this goes down very easily.

Posted from CellarTracker

posts.name: Bob Hughes
posts.cooked:
So as context, we do enjoy Sancerre in the Hughes household, and Boulay may be my favorite producer - certainly, by “volume consumed”, he is.

As I was typing in my TN in Eric’s wonderful database, I saw that there were 18 vintages of the Clos de Beaujeu in CT, and there are still 25 bottles of the '97 in inventory.

To be honest, I’ve always considered Sancerre to be a “ready when released” white - how does this wine change with age?

We opened one of our 2008s this last January, so six years old anyhow. SB is far from my favorite grape, so its favorable impression was definitely noticed. I don’t have notes but it was delicious, deep and complex, with not a bit of the grape’s tendency (to my palate, anyhow) towards rawness. So…I don’t really have many benchmarks, but certainly this wine did not taste anywhere near gone.

posts.name: John Gilman
posts.cooked:
Hi Bob,

I certainly age my Sancerre for extended periods of time, but mostly I cellar exclusively Edmond Vatan and Francois Cotat, and these wines are meant to age a long time. I try to age my Boulay bottles, but always seem to finish off the majority before they see a full year in the cellar (as the temptation is always to crack a Boulay and let the Cotat and Vatan sleep). I am looking forward to writing about how the Boulay wines age over a longer period of time in the coming year. As far as Vatan and Cotat, I am currently drinking (slowly, as neither vintage is fully mature) the 2007s from Francois Cotat and the 2002 from Monsieur Vatan. I still have a couple of bottles of the '97 Vatan in the cellar that I am trying to ignore, as it is a great vintage here and the wine has years of life ahead of it. What one has to remember is that in the village of Chavignol one finds the same outcropping of Kimmeridgian limestone as is found in the Grand Crus of Chablis, and which does not surface anywhere else in Sancerre. My gut feeling is that this is one of the main reasons that the longest-lived wines of Sancerre hail from producers based in Chavignol, but also the style of fermentation and elevage of these producers lends itself to longevity. The Vatan and Francois Cotat wines are fermented in very, very old foudres, rather than the stainless steel tanks fashionable at the vast majority of Sancerre estates. The wines go into bottle tight, structured and built for the long haul, so it really is best to age them- just like grand cru Chablis. Whether or not producers in other villages could attain the same longevity from their vineyards if they used similar fermentation techniques is an interesting question, as it may be the combination of the traditional style of vinification and their great terroir that produces such long-lived wines chez Vatan and Cotat. Both estates also crop much lower than the average in Sancerre. I am not (yet) as intimately familar with the cellar practices at Domaine Boulay, but am very much looking forward to immersing myself in a range of older vintages and seeing what the results may bring, as they are starting from that same great base of Kimmeridgian limestone.

All the Best,

John
I try to age them as best I can…I’m still cella ring a 2009 Dagueneau…but his wines are known to age even longer. I just can’t seem to buy enough to age for too long.

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: I’d like to be able to age Vatan, but I simply don’t wish to spend the money on them. So that leaves the Cotat Bros. to rely on. I like them with 6-8 years on them. Age mellows the acidity and melds the whole together. I tried 1 Boulay so far and was not impressed: it seemed simple and acidic. I would have rather have had an inexpensive Chablis for the same profile.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Count me as another fan of aged Sancerre, from the right producers. We had a 1994 Cotat Monts Damnés in July which was just lovely.

Over time, the wines’ youthful aromas of apple, lime and what John is wont to call ‘botanicals’ to segue into more honeyed aromas and flavors along the lines of custard apple and candied fruits, beeswax, petrol, etc. This can take a very long time, however. That 1994 Cotat was still very youthful, for example.

I’m also an admirer of Boulay’s. My experiences with his Monts Damnés have been consistently excellent.

One of the great frustrations of my cellar is that my modest stock of Vatan is strictly off-limits, as I’m trying to be disciplined about waiting 10+ years after the vintage before opening bottles. So I really only get to drink it in France off wine lists.

posts.name: Mike Grammer
posts.cooked:
You guys are better than me—I can’t keep my hands off the few Boulays and Cotats I get my paws on. Would love to see how a couple of these bottles age someday, but I’d have to leave them with a friend, maybe in a different province or something, in order to resist temptation. Almost never see Vatan unfortunately…

Have had a more or less lifelong love affair with Sancerre, right from the time I was about 14 and we went on a houseboat cruise down the Loire canal with the family and cycled up into Sancerre and right into a wine cellar or two.

Mike

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
I drink Didier Dagueneau Blanc Fume de Pouilly, maybe a couple bottles a year. I’ve been drinking them around age 5-7, and they seem to be very good at that age, lively yet complex.

I haven’t been drinking them on first release, so I couldn’t really say how different they are at that age, but they’re in a very good place at 5-7 years, and I’m sure for at least a few more if not longer.

posts.name: Chuck Anderson
posts.cooked: I aspire to leave at least a few of my 2012 & 13 Boulays long enough to see what happens.

posts.name: Joseph Rater
posts.cooked:
I’m holding a few bottles of 2007 and 2010 Boulay Monts Damnees for another five to ten years. The 2007
Monts was less fleshy, more mineral, tight and youthful compared to the 2007 Beaujeu a couple years ago. Picked up some of the 2014s as well.

posts.name: Mike Evans
posts.cooked: My 1998 and 1999 Thomas-Labaille Busters continue to drink well, and have picked up a hint of peppermint that is surprisingly enjoyable.
I really like Sancerre and treat it like Beaujolais; I drink them soon after purchase. Great input above, maybe I need to pick up a few more bottles and let them gather some dust.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
On one of my first visits with Gerard, he poured me a smidge of a Beaujeu from the early 90s that he said had already been open for two weeks.
I was amazed at its precision, linear drive, and unoxidized brightness. Its complexity was wowow impressive, certainly worth the transformative wait.
Indeed, these can go the distance.
I know that the Beaujeu and Damnés are vinified differently than the Cote and Comtesse, the latter two aged in wood, a small percentage of which is new. I will follow up with precise details, pour ne pas dire des betises.
An aside, Gerard is thrilled with the quality of his '15 rosé and “enthusiastic” about his '15 Sancerre Tradition. He ins’t one to unsolicited reach out to share his enthusiasms, so the fact that he did so for these shows just how geeked he is.
From Thomas Calder, his US agent:
"Gérard called me this morning to express his enthusiasm for the rosé 2015 which he is racking today.
It is very rare that Gérard calls me. He thinks the 2015 may be the best rosé he has ever made, “gourmand et vinous…vraiment très bien”.
“Gérard is also enthusiastic about the Tradition blanc. He says he picked at just the right moment to have a good balance. There were some domaines who waited and will have Sancerre weighing in at 16°. He compares it to 2005.”
FYI
:0
Disclaimer: You are your mother’s child.
Double disclaimer: Gerard is my friend.
Triple disclaimer: I import his wines. (with a refill of the '14 crus lined up to come with the '15 rosé in the springtime)

posts.name: Bob Hughes
posts.cooked:
I’ll be in for a case of the '15 rose

I still haven’t tried the '14 side-by-side with either the Pibarnon or the Vaccelli, but all three are very nice on their own.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
To continue in thread drift, the “new” Pibarnon rosé, les Nuances, arrives next week.
Psyched I am!
And the '14 Vaccelli Vaccelli rosé arrived a month or so ago.
I love how pink wine has a price ceiling threshold, no matter how good it is, $25-35 is the ceiling. To the savvy consumer’s benefit.

posts.name: Jeff Vaughan
posts.cooked: I liked the Vacceilli rose. How does the Pibarnon compare with it, Boulay and Montenidoli?

posts.name: Bob Hughes
posts.cooked:
It’s funny, Jeff, but for all the Montenidoli I get from Rob, I can’t recall trying her rose yet.

One thing about Pibarnon that I like is that their wines clearly handle time in the bottle well, so it’s not like you feel you need to drink every bottle the Summer you buy it.

Rob may disagree with me here, but I find Boulay a little less consistent than Pibarnon - some vintages I think their rose may be the best I’ve had all year, and other times they seem solid yet unexciting. So for that reason I give an edge to Pibarnon, but they are both top-notch producers, IMO.

posts.name: Robert Panzer
posts.cooked:
A bit apples and oranges.
Pibarnon is a)in toasty seaside Provence, b)Mourvèdre based, a thick skinned variety that needs a long time to ripen the tannin, with rich body/higher alc as a part of that. Then, the Cinsault is used for its freshness/acidity. The combo makes a more complete critter than either would allow for otherwise.
Boulay is way further north in the Loire, 100% Pinot Noir on limestone/clay. Much cooler, more marginal climate for red grapes.
I’d say both are quite consistent for what they are.
Perhaps a tipping point for either would be:
-cold/wet years in the loire where you have plenty of acid but not enough sugar
-excessively warm years in Provence, when perhaps there is too much sugar without achieving phenolic ripeness yet
Bob, you should Elisabetta’s rosé. Classy classy classy! I have a bottle of '14 for you if you want. It, too, is remarkably consistent.

posts.name: Bob Hughes
posts.cooked:
BTW, while I’ve been posting today I have had a glass of the 2011 Clos de Beaujeu beside me (nothing like a vacation day!).

That overt grassiness on the nose has resolved, but this wine is still more angular and “tense” than the '13 version.

fancy_title: TNs: Two from Boulay (and does anybody out there “age” their Sancerre?)
title: TNs: Two from Boulay (and does anybody out there "age" their Sancerre?)
